narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background, you've put in the effort to pursue law school, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70 and an LSAT score of 26.50, while planning to attend part-time. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 56% chance that you would fail the bar exam—tipping the scales just enough to reject your application, as the school prioritizes those predicted to pass on the first try. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5th or 6th decile and signaled weaker performance in later undergrad years. On a brighter note, your Year 1 decile of 6th—slightly above average—helped push the odds toward passing, showing early promise that the model appreciated. Your LSAT score, well below the dataset average of around 37, also contributed toward the failure prediction by raising concerns about standardized test readiness for the rigors ahead. Meanwhile, your undergraduate GPA, though under the typical 3.21, offered some counterbalance by nudging the prediction slightly toward success. Being in the part-time track, which is less common than full-time for most students, actually worked in your favor a bit, as did your middle-income bracket. Compared to typical applicants who pass the bar, your profile stands out for that sharp drop from strong early grades to a low Year 3 ranking, combined with the lower LSAT, which together outweighed your other strengths. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a person or lawyer—it's just the model's data-driven read on bar exam patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying with an updated profile that addresses these key areas. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by targeting these improvements, and we're rooting for you to turn this around. Keep pushing—you've got the foundation to build on.
